text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes that the small bowel limbs are not dilated and there are no visible signs of ischemia.
The surgeon locates the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) and begins to run the small bowel limbs, inspecting them for any abnormalities.
The surgeon observes the Roux limb, noting its retrocholic and retrogastric positioning, and confirms that it is fairly short with no visible signs of obstruction.
The surgeon runs the biliopancreatic limb, observing no obstruction up to the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon evaluates the gastric pouch, finding no abnormalities, and inspects Peterson's defect and the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) for signs of internal hernias, confirming none are present.
The surgeon inspects the common channel from the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) to the terminal ileum, observing no abnormalities or obstructions in the small bowel.
The surgeon observes laxity in the common channel, which appears to be extending retrograde into the jejunojejunostomy (JJ).
Contrast is observed traveling up the biliopancreatic limb, indicating patency and flow within this segment.
The surgeon places two permanent sutures along the bowel, ensuring alignment to prevent any kinks in the tissue.
